title: Dog Greyish Vaginal Discharge: Causes, Risks, and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Is greyish discharge in dogs an emergency? Learn why this symptom requires immediate veterinary care and how to triage the situation effectively.
content_html:
What is Greyish Vaginal Discharge in Dogs?

Greyish, cloudy, or milky vaginal discharge in dogs is typically an indication of a bacterial infection or the presence of inflammatory cells. Unlike the clear or slightly bloody discharge seen during a normal heat cycle, greyish discharge (often referred to as purulent discharge) suggests an accumulation of pus. This can be caused by localized conditions like vaginitis, or more systemic and dangerous conditions involving the reproductive tract.

Urgency Level: High

If you notice greyish discharge, the urgency level is considered high. For intact (unspayed) female dogs, this is a hallmark sign of pyometra, a life-threatening uterine infection that requires emergency surgery. Even if your dog appears to be acting normally, internal infections can escalate into sepsis within hours. Do not wait for symptoms like lethargy or vomiting to appear; contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

When you contact a veterinary clinic, providing a clear photo of the discharge can significantly speed up the triage process. A visual allows the medical team to evaluate the color, opacity, and consistency of the fluid. This helps them distinguish between mild localized irritation and signs of a critical systemic infection, ensuring your dog receives the appropriate priority level upon arrival at the hospital.

faqs.question: Can I wait until my regular vet opens tomorrow?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to wait. If the discharge is caused by pyometra, the condition is time-sensitive and can be fatal if the uterus ruptures or the infection enters the bloodstream.

faqs.question: What if my dog is spayed but has grey discharge?
faqs.answer: While less common, spayed dogs can still experience vaginitis, severe urinary tract infections, or 'stump pyometra' if any uterine tissue remains. A veterinary exam is still necessary to rule out these issues.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms to look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for excessive licking of the area, increased thirst, frequent urination, a bloated abdomen, or a sudden loss of appetite, all of which indicate a serious underlying infection.
